subtitle: eCommerce & Agile Delivery
hero_sub: End-to-end delivery. Release governance. Marketplace operations. Twenty years of doing exactly this.

the_case.answer: 20+ years owning scope, timelines, dependencies, and outcomes across eCommerce and platform delivery
the_case.detail: Every role I've held for two decades has been end-to-end ownership. Not coordination support — full accountability for scope, schedule, risk, and outcome. Eptura, Meta, Understood.org, A&E. Same job, different scale.

the_case.answer: Onboarded Amazon FBA for a boutique publisher. Feed errors, listing misconfigs, inventory sync failures — firsthand.
the_case.detail: Every failure mode in the Rithum playbook — wrong-language listings appearing in markets where the product wasn't available, oversells from inventory sync lag, feed formatting rejections — I navigated all of it in a real production environment with real business consequences.

the_case.answer: Deep familiarity with how data moves across the ecosystem, where it breaks, and what the PM does when it does.
the_case.detail: I know the stack end-to-end: SAP as source of truth, OIC as the translation layer, SFCC for DTC, Rithum for marketplace syndication, OMS for order routing. I know which node owns which failure and how to sequence the fix.

the_case.need: Cross-functional coordination: Engineering, QA, Product, IT, vendors, regional teams
the_case.answer: This is the job I've done for 20 years. Eptura: ~10 distributed teams. A&E: 12-15 concurrent release streams.
the_case.detail: I don't just attend cross-functional meetings — I run them. At Meta I coordinated across creative, engineering, and QA across 14+ countries. At A&E I kept 10+ concurrent workstreams aligned across Engineering, QA, UX, Content, and Marketing.

the_case.need: RAID logs, release artifacts, delivery documentation
the_case.answer: Built RAID logs, release runbooks, deployment checklists, and status dashboards from scratch across every engagement.
the_case.detail: The deliverables are the job. RAID logs updated daily, not at retrospective. Status reports that go out before anyone has to ask. Release artifacts that the QA team can run without a PM in the room.

the_case.need: QA coordination, defect triage, post-launch stabilization
the_case.answer: Led QA coordination and post-launch stabilization across platform releases at Understood.org, Meta, and A&E.
the_case.detail: QA is where releases are won or lost. I've run defect triage bridges, managed release readiness gates, and owned post-launch stabilization sprints. At Understood.org I landed a complex replatform with no critical regressions. At Crocs I'll bring the same discipline.

the_case.need: Agile delivery in a fast-paced environment
the_case.answer: CSM certified. 10+ years running Agile ceremonies for delivery teams in high-pressure environments.
the_case.detail: Agile isn't a process I follow — it's how I think. I run clean standups, maintain healthy backlogs, and use retrospectives to actually improve the process. At Eptura I drove 35%+ delivery efficiency gains through process standardization. I don't just certify Agile — I produce results with it.

the_stack.headline: The eCommerce ecosystem.
the_stack.subhead: I know where data moves, where it breaks, and who owns the fix.

nodes.label: SAP
nodes.desc: Source of truth for inventory, pricing, and order data across the enterprise
nodes.pm_role: Coordinates timing for data pushes across downstream platforms; first escalation point for sync failures that originate in ERP

nodes.label: OIC Middleware
nodes.desc: Translation and routing layer between SAP and downstream platforms
nodes.pm_role: Manages deployment sequencing and monitors for integration errors during releases; owns the dependency map between SAP and SFCC/Rithum

nodes.label: Salesforce Commerce Cloud (SFCC)
nodes.desc: DTC storefront and digital experience platform for global and regional markets
nodes.pm_role: Release coordination for storefront deployments; QA gate ownership through UAT and cutover; post-launch stabilization point of contact

nodes.label: Rithum (ChannelAdvisor)
nodes.desc: Marketplace syndication platform managing product feeds to Amazon, regional marketplaces, and third-party channels
nodes.pm_role: Monitors feed health, coordinates feed failure response, sequences marketplace launches with ops and content teams

nodes.label: OMS / Order Management
nodes.desc: Order routing, fulfillment coordination, and inventory allocation across channels
nodes.pm_role: Coordinates OMS-impacting releases with fulfillment and ops; manages rollback sequencing when order flow is at risk

when_things_break.scenario: Rithum feed failure → Amazon listing suppression
response: Pull Rithum feed logs within 15 minutes of alert
Identify failure mode: format error, auth issue, or data problem at source
Coordinate with Integration and Marketplace Ops simultaneously — not sequentially
Draft stakeholder update with diagnosis and ETA before anyone asks for one
Own the resolution timeline; close the loop with a post-mortem within 24 hours

when_things_break.scenario: SFCC checkout defect discovered post-deploy in regional market
response: Confirm scope: single market or multi-region; quantify revenue impact
Convene defect triage bridge with Engineering and QA — not a status call, a war room
Assess rollback feasibility vs. hotfix timeline; make the recommendation, don't defer it
Brief regional stakeholder immediately with facts, not speculation
Hold the team to the fix timeline; own the go/no-go for any remediation deploy

when_things_break.scenario: SAP → OIC integration failure mid-release cycle
response: Stop downstream releases until the integration root cause is confirmed
Coordinate with SAP team, OIC team, and impacted platform owners in parallel
Maintain a live dependency map — who is blocked, for how long, what unblocks them
Keep leadership informed with a single source of truth, not competing updates
Sequence the restart once the fix is validated — not before

how_i_run_a_room: Every meeting has a stated purpose and ends with owner, action, and date — no exceptions
Daily standup with written recap and action items distributed same day
RAID log updated proactively — risks surface before they become problems
Stakeholder updates go out before anyone has to ask for one
Defect triage bridges are war rooms, not status calls — I come with a diagnosis, not just data
Release readiness is a gate I enforce, not a checkbox I collect
Post-mortems happen within 48 hours of any significant incident — not when schedules allow

points: End-to-end commerce platform build from zero: WordPress, WooCommerce, Stripe, ApplePay, Printify API
Vendor coordination across design, development, and fulfillment
AI-assisted delivery workflows embedded throughout the build-to-ship pipeline
Reduced order-to-fulfillment cycle time 25% through workflow redesign
~10 distributed teams across India and California; PI-aligned SAFe environment
35%+ delivery efficiency gain through process standardization and coaching
20% sprint carryover reduction through readiness checks and WIP discipline
ADO and Jira delivery dashboards maintained for leadership visibility
Distressed program recovery: Sitecore-to-Contentful replatform, final crucial months
Globally distributed team across 12+ countries; regulated, healthcare-adjacent environment
On-time launch with no critical release regressions
WCAG 2.1 AA compliance enforced as a core production requirement
12–15 concurrent release streams across Web, iOS, tvOS, Android, Roku
Coordinated Engineering, QA, UX, Content, and Marketing across NY, India, Poland, Egypt
Migrated teams from ad-hoc tracking to fully implemented Jira
Shipped all platforms against externally visible launch windows

before: You're managing delivery AND writing status reports AND triaging vendor escalations — all simultaneously
Every release needs your direct involvement to move forward, even the routine ones
Stakeholders ping you directly for updates because there's no reliable alternative path
Risks surface late — after they've already impacted the timeline
after: You get a weekly status update you can forward to leadership unchanged
Routine releases move without you unless there's an actual decision needed from your level
Stakeholders have a single point of contact who keeps them current and accurate
Risks surface early, with a recommended response — not just a flag

the_ramp.deliverable: Access to Jira, Smartsheet, Confluence, ADO. Platform walkthroughs with Engineering and QA leads. Stakeholder intros scheduled.
Shadow all active workstreams. Audit current RAID log and open risks. Map all in-flight dependencies across SFCC, Rithum, and OMS.
Own one active workstream end-to-end. Daily sync with Sr. PM. First status update drafted and circulated.
Full ownership of assigned initiatives. Leadership-ready status reporting in place. No handholding needed — just clear escalation paths.
First retrospective on my own delivery. Process improvements identified. Ready to absorb additional scope.
